title: Miles 0-77 (Julian, CA)
text: A perfect start in the PCT high desert with stunning vistas and daytime highs rarely reaching above 70. Starting with multiple 20 mile days, we're restraining our excitement to push on with the need to start easy to reduce the chance of injury.

title: Miles 77-152 (Idyllwild, CA)
text: The trail remains windy and cool. Cactus-filled hillsides turn into dry rocky meadows before climbs to forested mountaintops. After back to back 26 mile days, we took a "zero day" in town today to resupply, rest, and prepare snow gear for the ascent up Mt. San Jacinto later this week.

title: Miles 153-209 (Palm Springs, CA)
text: A mountain adventure full of alpine views, switchbacks, and climbs. We "cowboy camped" under the stars at 9000 ft on Thursday night and woke up at 3am to climb to the top of the 10,800ft Mt. San Jacinto for sunrise. We then descended down to 1200 ft, reaching the Interstate 10 freeway at 9pm. The two day sprint was concluded with a pickup from a friend who kindly drove out from LA, followed by nighttime burritos + margaritas on the Palm Springs strip.

title: Miles 210-266 (Big Bear, CA)
text: We walked from the desert valley floor into the San Bernardino mountains before reaching the beautiful sands and canyons of the Whitewater Preserve. An 8,000 ft ascent day followed before reaching the ski town of Big Bear for resupply and laundry. Also had the honor of carrying #pctbabyyoda2021 before handing him off safely to @getfknlost. May the 4th be with you!

title: Miles 267-363 (Wrightwood, CA)
text: We continue traveling through a blend of mountain passes and alpine ridges, officially passing latitude above LA. The trail followed the Mojave river, which was an oasis in the dry hills for several days. A delicious pizza delivery at Silverwood Lake was followed by a half day of rest yesterday in the friendly mountain town of Wrightwood.

title: Miles 364-478 (Green Valley, CA)
text: The scenery changes and temps rise as the PCT descends out of the San Gabriel Mountains towards the Mojave desert. We endured an exposed 23 mile roadwalk to bypass the Bobcat Fire trail closure, through which the smell of burned lingered from last October's fire. Other challenges have also emerged including bushwhacking through trail covered face high in poison oak and full day water carries. In addition, I developed "thats the biggest blister I've ever seen" (according to several other hikers) on my heel but elected not to pop the golf ball sized growth until off-trail due to the risk of infection. We took a zero day yesterday and I am taking at least another day off today, a frustrating but hopefully prudent decision. Learning lesson: putting a bandaid on your foot creates big blisters!

title: Miles 479-566 (Tehachapi, CA)
text: With 60 mph wind gusts and no natural cover, this past section through the Mojave was one of the most notorious parts of the PCT. 17 miles of it was walking along the LA aqueduct, the water source for 13 million people. On the topic of engineering marvels, walking amidst wind turbines the height of the Statue of Liberty was exhilarating (they're loud!). In order to get through the desert quicker, the last ~50 miles were completed in one day as part of the famous "Tehachapi Challenge." In town the 3 of us regrouped as I had been a day behind due to last week's blister. After getting a generous ride from a local "trail angel," we're now back on trail for the final days in Southern California.

title: Miles 567-702 (Kennedy Meadows, CA)
text: We've made it to the biggest milestone so far on trail: the gateway to the snowy Sierras. Because there were so few water sources on this backcountry section, hikers are grateful for two caches of water jugs resupplied by a local resident. Afterwards the landscape quickly changed to pine groves and granite rocks, a contrast from the Joshua trees of the desert. Once reaching the rural outpost of Kennedy Meadows, we replaced worn shoes and received bear cans + snow gear from resupply boxes (thanks Mom for mailing mine!). We then got an incredibly generous 2-hour ride to and from the town of Kernville from the mom of a fellow hiker for a relaxing day off, along with a much needed stop at a laundromat. We're now excited to be heading into the mountains, starting with Sequoia National Park.

title: Miles 703-789 (Bishop, CA)
text: We spent the past week in the remote southern Sierra Nevada range. This magical stretch of the PCT overlaps the John Muir Trail, often regarded as the most scenic hiking trail in the country. Almost the entire week was spent above 10,000 ft. We took a side trip to wake up at 12:30 am to see a stunning sunrise from the top of Mt. Whitney, which at 14,505 feet is the highest point in the USA outside of Alaska. The following day we crossed Forrester Pass, the highest point on the Pacific Crest Trail. A steep 7.5 mile hike off the trail followed to reach the nearest road at Onion Valley Trailhead. All of the day hikers there smelled like laundry detergent, but surely they said much worse things about how we smelled! We're now headed back into the mountains with 40lb packs for another weeklong carry in the wilderness.

title: Mammoth Lakes, CA
text: The sensory overload of the High Sierra continues. This stretch consisted of 5-10 mile climbs up snowy passes followed by descents of similar lengths into lush valleys. Most nights we camped between 10k and 11k feet to both escape mosquitos and hike on mountain snow when it's hard in the early morning. Water was everywhere and the fish seemed as abundant as the nighttime stars. We caught a dozen trout from alpine lakes (all legally with a CA fishing permit) and fried them up to make delicious fish tacos. Thanks Mom for recommending the olive oil and lime juice for cooking! With only seeing ~15 other backpackers per day on trail, the contrast was immediate upon arriving at the Motel 6 in the city of Mammoth. After burrito-filled resupply we got a ride back to Horseshoe Lake trailhead from @_teambeaver, who works on supplying cable to the area's cell phone towers on which we have been so dependent throughout the hike. Miles 790-903 of the PCT.

title: Tahoe City, CA
text: We've completed the final remote segment through the High Sierra. While it's sad the stunning mountain sights will soon end, we're also excited for relief from the swarms of mosquitos, shoe soaking river crossings, and 14-hour hiking days. The journey north wove a path through Ansel Adams Wilderness, Yosemite National Park, and Desolation Wilderness. It was awesome to have college friends join us on the trail during parts of the latter two sections. They also kindly brought resupply boxes! However the news hasn't all been good, as Evan pulled his hamstring on a steep snowbank and has had to take several days off trail to recover. Fortunately, the injury timing coincides with a relaxing halftime break we had planned for my parents to visit in Lake Tahoe. Miles 904-1135 of the PCT.

title: Truckee, CA
text: During our 10-day break, I had an incredible time hiking with my parents around the Tahoe area and then spending several burrito-filled days in San Francisco. Shout out to my Dad for coming along on a mosquito-filled 27 mile hike at 9,000 feet! We're now back with light packs and marching towards Oregon. It's hot and dry again. There is a wildfire a couple hundred miles north of us which we will be closely monitoring - so far the trail remains open. It was an exceptionally dry winter, which means a greater threat of fires will be present for the remainder of the summer. Evan's hamstring is healed, but during that time I developed a slight pain in my right knee from the heavy biking/running in the Bay Area - in hindsight a poor decision on my part. As such, the break was prolonged a couple extra days to reduce the risk of my getting stuck with an injury in the upcoming rural stretch of trail. Between fires and injuries, not every day is smooth sailing on the PCT but we went into the adventure knowing that would be the case. It's great to be back! Miles 1136-1152 of the PCT.

title: Burney, CA
text: In track I always found the third quarter of the race to be the toughest and the same is proving to be true for the PCT. The past week in Northern California has been the hottest yet on trail, topping out at 107 degrees on exposed ridges and barren valleys. Also saw 3 bears in the last week, but fortunately they were too far away to get a good picture. The knee pain I developed during our time off trail put me a couple days behind Sam and Evan and I wasn't able to catch back up to them as quickly as planned due to the risk of re-injuring it. They took a day off to wait for me and we're all thrilled to be back together. Am continuing to be careful with the knee to reduce the chance of it taking me off trail for an extended time period. On the other hand, it has been awesome to run into so many hikers we had been with earlier on. The terrain has flattened out and it has been rewarding to do 30 to 35 miles each day. During brief stops through small friendly trail towns we didn't have much energy to do much more besides ruminate about the heat and drink cold root beers. We also walked through the geysers and bubbling hot springs of the beautiful Lassen Volcanic National Park. Miles 1152-1411 of the PCT.

title: Miles 1412-1656 (Seiad Valley, CA)
text: The heat wave has passed and the miles are flying by as we hike through the rural mountains of Northern California. We didn't realize how beautiful it is up here! For the past week, "The Three Amigos" (as we're known on trail) has grown to five with Jenny and Jack hiking with us. Along the way, we had an incredible stay at Barbara's house in the town of Mt. Shasta. Evan met her in the post office and she invited us over for showers, BBQ dinner, and to spend the night. We're now departing the final trail town in CA, with the forests of Oregon lying just beyond a final mountain ridge.

title: Miles 1657-1718 (Ashland, OR)
text: And that's a state! We reached the California/Oregon border, an exciting milestone. Since we walked roads around trail closures in the state, we were able to record a continuous footpath across California. Fortunately the massive Bootleg Fire in Southern Oregon is not currently a threat to the PCT. The summer wild flowers are in full bloom. The trail has become greener, cooler, and flatter - all welcome changes after the ruggedness of NorCal.

title: Miles 1719-2001 (Bend, OR)
text: Although Oregon is called the "Green Tunnel," it regularly opens into stunning panoramas of Central Cascades volcanoes. The days have been long: 35 to 40 miles of hiking, typically waking up at 4:30am and going until 8pm. The group went back down to 4 as Jenny left trail to attend a wedding back home. We were met with some unexpected thunderstorms in the beautiful Sisters Wilderness - and were quite unprepared for it since we shipped back tents in Tahoe to shed weight. I had an uncomfortable night in my emergency survival bivvy. Going tentless "seemed like a good idea at the time" but it's great to now have them back. The incredible kindness of local town residents has also continued as we're regularly asked if we'd like rides, cold drinks, and food. They never get turned down!

title: Miles 2002-2230 (Trout Lake, WA)
text: We crossed the dramatically named "Bridge of the Gods" over the Columbia River into the final state on the hike. The trail has changed once again to lush rainforests full of ferns and moss. Hikers compete with the bears to eat delicious wild berries, slowing down our mile pace considerably. In Northern Oregon, the PCT was closed due to fire but because of road closures it required a 6 hour driving detour that extended all the way to Portland's suburbs - all to bypass 50 miles of trail. As a result I got to see my cousin Stacie and attend a fellow hiker's birthday party in the city. After the closure we were met with the stunning Mt. Hood section of the PCT. Included was a stop at the historic Timberline Lodge, made famous by the movie The Shining. 420 miles of the Northern Cascades remain until the Canadian border.

title: Miles 2231-2465 (Stevens Pass, WA)
text: Many hikers say Washington is their favorite section of the trail and we're beginning to see why. Beautiful jagged alpine ridges have panoramic views of the region's volcanoes - Mt. St Helens, Mt. Adams, and the imposing Mt. Rainier. However the stunning views vanished for several days due to a new nearby wildfire. It sent the air quality plummeting and we had to hike in masks for protection from the ash. Fortunately it never threatened us on the trail. The past week has been physically challenging due to the steep and rocky trail. It was a refreshing break to stay a night at our friend Peggy's cozy mountain home. We met Peggy last month when she was hiking the Northern California section. She graciously offered to host us when we reached Snoqualmie Pass.

title: Miles 2466-2573 (Stehekin, WA)
text: Last Thursday my Dad survived a stroke and fortunately is in stable condition. My Mom's quick thinking allowed him to get to the hospital immediately, where doctors were prepared for emergency surgery to clear the blockage. His strength has been noticeably improving every day, and we are grateful for the amazing support he's had. Bad news never has good timing. While most of the trail has been remarkably accessible, the North Cascades are not. I found out via satellite phone in the town of Stehekin. As the most remote stop on the PCT, it has no road access to the outside world - only a 4 hour ferry. The boat ride out was followed by a gracious 3.5 hour drive to Seattle from my Mom's cousins Bob and Janet. Then a red eye flight to Minneapolis. Looking forward to completing (and sharing pictures of) the final 81 miles of the PCT another time, but my Dad's recovery is the number one priority. It's great to be around his positivity and jokes in the hospital. The guys are finishing at the Canadian border today, but right now there's no place I'd rather be than home.

title: Miles 2573-2573 (Stehekin pt. 2)
text: It's great to be back in Washington. My Dad is recovering well from the stroke, having already gained much of his lost functionality. At home he has already been able to grill and do light gym workouts. I'm glad to have been with him in the hospital and during his initial intense rehab. Just watching the therapy was as tiring as hiking the PCT! Pics above are of the miles we did coming into Stehekin last month, which were some of the most rugged but beautiful ones yet. I'm excited to savor this week's final 81 miles as well.

title: Miles 2573-2654 (Manning Park, BC)
text: And that's a country! I tagged the Canadian border on 9/9 and celebrated with a packed out can of Washington cider (worth the weight). The trail was different than that of 3 weeks ago. The ever-present berries and wildflowers have disappeared, replaced with a full spectrum of fall colors. The air was crisp and cool. Because hiking into Canada is prohibited due to Covid, those final 81 miles were followed by a 30 mile "victory lap" south to the nearest road. Many fist bumps were had. It was exciting to see several hikers we had been with early in California. I camped at 7,000ft on a ridge with 360 degree views of surrounding glaciers and peaks - a picture perfect final night on trail before a rainy last day of hiking. Friday night celebrations in the small town of Mazama and a long weekend in Seattle mark a fun-filled conclusion to this adventure of a lifetime.
